_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Radiation Exposure. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Radiation Exposure

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Radiation Exposure
points: Prioritize identification and treatment of immediately life-threatening medical conditions and traumatic injuries above any radiation-associated injury
Identify and appropriately treat acute radiation injury
Reduce risk for contamination of personnel while caring for patients potentially or known to be contaminated with radioactive material

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients who have been acutely exposed to ionizing radiation from accidental environmental release of a radioactive source
Patients who have been acutely exposed to ionizing radiation from a non-accidental environmental release of a radioactive source
Patients who have been contaminated with material emitting ionizing radiation
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Patients exposed to normal doses of ionizing radiation from medical imaging studies
Patients exposed to normal doses of ionizing radiation from therapeutic medical procedures

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Treat life-threatening medical problems and traumatic injuries prior to assessing for and treating radiation injuries or performing decontamination

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Irradiated patients pose no threat to medical clinicians
Contaminated patients pose very little threat to medical clinicians who use appropriate PPE including N95 masks or respirators, gloves, gowns, and face and eye protection
Sources of radiation a. Legal i. Industrial plants

Physiology of radiation poisoning c. Contamination: Poisoning from direct exposure to a radioactive source, contaminated debris, liquids, or clothing where radiation continues to be emitted from particles on surface
Common types of radioactivity that cause poisoning e. Gamma rays i. Highest frequency of ionizing rays
In general, trauma patients who have been exposed to or contaminated by radiation should be triaged and treated based on the severity of their conventional injuries

A patient who is contaminated with radioactive material (i.e., flecks of radioactive material embedded in their clothing and skin) generally poses a minimal exposure risk to medical personnel, although should not be placed in a contained space before decontamination
EMS clinicians may be asked to assist public health agencies in the distribution and

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Duration of exposure to the radioactive source or environment
' Distance (if able to be determined) from the radioactive source (if known)
' Time of onset of vomiting

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Treat life-threatening medical problems and traumatic injuries prior to assessing for and treating radiation injuries or performing decontamination
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
